ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 represents crab as a food item (whole). No Sri Lanka-specific crab entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJK covers crabs as a category. No entry specific to Sri Lankan green crab or Scylla serrata; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 47492001 represents crab as a substance. No Sri Lanka-specific entry; closest_parent used.

preparation_method: typically steamed, stir-fried, or cooked in sauce at restaurant
main_ingredients: whole crab (Scylla serrata or Portunus pelagicus)
no added ingredients at raw stage

serving.description: 1 whole crab, approximately 1 kg live weight
serving.natural_language_portion: One whole Sri Lanka crab of approximately 1 kg live weight, yielding roughly 250–350 g of edible meat after shell removal, typically shared among 2–4 diners at a Zi Char or seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15139); cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods 'Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat'
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sri Lanka green crab (Scylla serrata / Portunus pelagicus) is not individually listed in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked blue crab (NDB 15139) as the closest approved analogue for edible crab meat. Nutrient values represent edible meat portion per 100 g. Whole-crab live weight includes substantial inedible shell; edible yield is approximately 25–35% of live weight. IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA do not publish a specific Sri Lankan green crab entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this analogue.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — shellfish and protein-dominant seafood category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Crab meat contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g edible portion). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature classify protein-dominant shellfish and crustaceans as having an effectively zero glycemic response. glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) per Veritas convention for foods with no glycemic carbohydrates. gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0 g per 100 g edible meat. GL per 1 kg whole crab (approx. 300 g edible meat) = (1 × 0 × 300) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 395 mg per 100 g edible crab meat; restaurant preparation (sauces, salt, seasoning) substantially increases sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume plain steamed or lightly prepared crab in modest portions (100–150 g edible meat) and request minimal added salt or sauce, as restaurant-style Zi Char preparations can raise sodium intake well above 600 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0 g per 100 g edible meat) and high protein (19.4 g per 100 g) produce no postprandial glycemic spike
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sri Lanka crab meat is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its zero glycemic load; however, diabetic diners should avoid high-sugar sauces (e.g., sweet chilli, black bean with added sugar) commonly used in Zi Char crab preparations, which can significantly raise the glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (approximately 100 mg per 100 g edible meat) and sodium; however, low saturated fat (0.3 g per 100 g) and beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids are positive factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab meat is relatively low in saturated fat and provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids, making it a heart-healthier seafood choice; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g edible meat and avoid high-sodium, high-fat sauces to keep overall dietary cholesterol and sodium within recommended limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sri Lanka Crab (1 kg whole) is a premium live or freshly caught crab dish served at Malaysian-style Zi Char and seafood restaurants in Singapore, most notably at establishments such as Marina Seafood House. The crabs are sourced from Sri Lanka — typically green crabs (Portunus pelagicus) or mud crabs (Scylla serrata) — prized for their sweet, firm, and succulent meat. The 1 kg designation refers to the live weight of the whole crab; edible meat yield is approximately 25–35% of live weight (250–350 g), making it a dish typically shared among 2–4 diners. Preparation methods vary by restaurant and customer preference: common styles include steamed with egg white, stir-fried with ginger and spring onion, cooked in black pepper sauce, chilli crab style, or butter milk preparation. The crab meat is nutritionally dense, providing approximately 102 kcal, 19.4 g protein, and only 2.0 g fat per 100 g edible portion, with negligible carbohydrate content. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (9.8 mcg per 100 g), selenium (40 mcg), zinc (3.8 mg), and phosphorus (239 mg). The glycemic index is effectively zero, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces. Sodium content in the raw crab is moderate (~395 mg per 100 g), but restaurant preparations with sauces can significantly increase sodium load, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. At SGD 118.80 per kilogram, this is a premium seafood item positioned in the upper tier of Singapore's Zi Char dining segment.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sri Lanka crab?
Can diabetics eat Sri Lanka crab?
Is Sri Lanka crab high in cholesterol?
What is the protein content of crab?
How much edible meat in a 1 kg crab?
What is the difference between Sri Lanka crab and mud crab?
Best way to cook Sri Lanka crab in Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked blue crab (the closest approved analogue) as no specific Sri Lankan green crab (Scylla serrata / Portunus pelagicus) entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the time of processing. The adaptation is scientifically defensible as crab species share broadly similar macronutrient profiles, but species-level variation in fat, mineral, and micronutrient content introduces moderate uncertainty. Confidence is therefore 'medium' rather than 'high'.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the edible crab meat portion per 100 g, not the whole live crab including shell. The 1 kg product weight is live/whole weight; edible yield is approximately 250–350 g. 2. carbohydrate_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked crab meat; crab meat contains no digestible starch or sugar. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per the same source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as crab is an animal-origin food with no plant fibre. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (sentinel for effectively zero GI) per Veritas convention for protein-dominant foods with no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry used as the primary analogue source. 5. Restaurant preparation (sauces, seasoning, butter, chilli) will substantially alter the final nutrient profile of the dish as served; values here represent the raw/plain cooked crab meat baseline. 6. The food_name in the input ('Sri Lanka Crab 1 Kg') has been normalised to 'Sri Lanka Crab (Whole, 1 kg)' for canonical English identity. 7. Halal status is marked true as crab is generally considered halal in the Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence followed in Singapore and Malaysia, though some schools differ; consumers should verify with the serving restaurant.
